Key Threats: BIOS/UEFI and EFI Attacks: Attackers target low-level firmware on Windows (BIOS/UEFI) and macOS (EFI) endpoints to install bootkit rootkits that persist across OS reinstallation and evade all OS-layer security controls.
MITRE ID: T1542.001 – Pre-OS Boot: System Firmware
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Supply Chain Compromise: Maliciously modified or backdoored firmware introduced during device manufacturing or logistics before deployment to the end user.
MITRE ID: T1195.003 – Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Hardware Supply Chain
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Outdated Firmware: Failure to apply firmware updates leaves known vulnerabilities exploitable, particularly relevant for Intel-based Macs and Windows endpoints from multiple OEMs with inconsistent patching cadences.
MITRE ID: T1068 – Exploitation for Privilege Escalation; T1542.001 – Pre-OS Boot: System Firmware
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Secure Boot / Apple Secure Boot Bypass: Disabling or circumventing Secure Boot (Windows) or Apple Secure Boot (macOS T2/Apple Silicon) permits unsigned or malicious bootloaders to execute prior to OS loading.
MITRE ID: T1542.001 – Pre-OS Boot: System Firmware; T1542.003 – Pre-OS Boot: Bootkit
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: TPM / Secure Enclave Tampering: On Windows, attackers attempt to extract TPM-sealed BitLocker keys via firmware TPM vulnerabilities (CVE-2023-1017/1018). On macOS, exploitation of T2 chip vulnerabilities may allow bypass of encrypted storage and activation lock protections.
MITRE ID: T1542.001 – Pre-OS Boot: System Firmware; T1600 – Weaken Encryption
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Firmware Persistence via UEFI Implants: Sophisticated threat actors (e.g., nation-state) deploy UEFI implants (MosaicRegressor, CosmicStrand-style) that survive disk wipes and OS reinstallation.
MITRE ID: T1542.001 – Pre-OS Boot: System Firmware; T1547 – Boot or Logon Autostart Execution
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: BMC / Remote Management Firmware Compromise: Baseboard Management Controller (BMC) or iDRAC firmware compromise on endpoints with remote management capabilities enables out-of-band persistent access.
MITRE ID: T1542.001 – Pre-OS Boot: System Firmware; T1133 – External Remote Services
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Device Theft Without Pre-Boot Authentication: Stolen laptops without full-disk encryption (BitLocker; FileVault) and a pre-boot PIN or password can be accessed by mounting the drive externally or booting to a live OS. Risk is elevated for employees who disable pre-boot PIN for convenience.
MITRE ID: T1052 – Exfiltration Over Physical Medium; T1005 – Data from Local System
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device; Data" → "Devices; Data" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: BadUSB and Peripheral Injection: Malicious USB devices (USB Rubber Ducky, O.MG Cable, Bash Bunny) emulate trusted HID devices such as keyboards and network adapters to inject keystrokes or exfiltrate data. Effective against both Windows and macOS with no driver installation required and no user prompt generated.
MITRE ID: T1200 – Hardware Additions; T1091 – Replication Through Removable Media; T1059 – Command and Scripting Interpreter; T1003 – OS Credential Dumping
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Thunderbolt and DMA Attacks: Thunderbolt ports on both Windows and macOS allow Direct Memory Access (DMA) at the hardware level, enabling tools such as PCILeech to read and write physical memory and extract credentials or encryption keys from a locked device without OS interaction. Mitigated by Kernel DMA Protection (Windows) and enabling Thunderbolt security levels; verify enforcement via MDM policy.
MITRE ID: T1006 – Direct Volume Access; T1003 – OS Credential Dumping
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Cold Boot Attack: An attacker with brief physical access reboots a running device and recovers remanent DRAM contents including encryption keys or session tokens. Apple Silicon endpoints implement hardware memory encryption rendering this attack practically infeasible. This threat remains active for Intel-based Windows and Mac endpoints without memory overwrite-on-suspend controls.
MITRE ID: T1542 – Pre-OS Boot; T1200 – Hardware Additions
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Evil Maid Attack: An unattended endpoint in a hotel room, conference room, or shared workspace can have its bootloader or firmware replaced, or a hardware keylogger implanted, with no user notification or tamper evidence on standard devices.
MITRE ID: T1056.001 – Input Capture: Keylogging; T1056.002 – Input Capture: GUI Input Capture; T1542 – Pre-OS Boot
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Shoulder Surfing and Visual Eavesdropping: Credentials, financial data, or customer PII visible on-screen in open-plan offices, airports, or client sites. Particularly high risk for employees without privacy screen filters. AI-assisted video analysis tools available as of 2025-2026 make this threat more scalable with commercially available equipment.
MITRE ID: T1552 – Unsecured Credentials; T1005 – Data from Local System

Key Threats: Insecure Device Disposal: Decommissioned endpoints not subjected to certified secure erasure (NIST SP 800-88 Rev.1) may retain cached credentials, browser sessions, banking application data, or sensitive files recoverable with commercially available forensic tools.
MITRE ID: T1005 – Data from Local System; T1552 – Unsecured Credentials
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Data; Device" → "Data; Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: PoE-Based Hardware Implants: Power-over-Ethernet inline implants or malicious docking station hardware installed on docked endpoints in open-plan trading floors or unmonitored desk locations, enabling persistent network interception or hardware-level keylogging without requiring access to the endpoint operating system.
MITRE ID: T1200 – Hardware Additions; T1040 – Network Sniffing; T1056.001 – Input Capture: Keylogging
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device; Network" → "Devices; Networks" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Security Feature Misconfiguration: Disabled or weakened OS protections — including Secure Boot, Credential Guard, Virtualisation-Based Security (VBS), and Hypervisor-Protected Code Integrity (HVCI) on Windows, or System Integrity Protection (SIP) and FileVault on macOS — frequently result from IT helpdesk workarounds, application compatibility exceptions, or unmanaged endpoints. Absence of VBS/HVCI should be treated as a device compliance failure and a Zero-Trust policy signal.
MITRE ID: T1562.001 – Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools; T1562.010 – Impair Defenses: Downgrade Attack
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Unpatched Operating System: Endpoints running outdated Windows or macOS versions expose known, publicly documented CVEs. Apple patches the three most recent major macOS releases for critical vulnerabilities; devices running versions older than N-2 receive no security updates and should be treated as non-compliant under device health policy.
MITRE ID: T1203 – Exploitation for Client Execution; T1068 – Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Kernel and Driver Exploitation: Vulnerabilities in third-party kernel extensions (kexts on macOS) or Windows kernel-mode drivers are exploited to achieve ring-0 privileges, bypassing all userland security controls including EDR agents and application sandboxes.
MITRE ID: T1068 – Exploitation for Privilege Escalation; T1014 – Rootkit
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Living-Off-The-Land Abuse on Windows: Legitimate Windows binaries (PowerShell, WMI, certutil, mshta, regsvr32, rundll32, msiexec, bitsadmin) abused to download, decode, and execute malicious code without writing new executables to disk, evading signature-based and hash-based detection. WDAC and AppLocker bypass via MSIX/AppX packaged applications is an active gap on Windows 10/11 endpoints.
MITRE ID: T1218 – System Binary Proxy Execution; T1059.001 – Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell; T1047 – Windows Management Instrumentation; T1647 – Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Living-Off-The-Land Abuse on macOS: Legitimate macOS binaries (osascript, curl, python3, bash, launchctl, installer, xattr, ditto) abused to execute payloads, establish persistence via LaunchAgents and LaunchDaemons, and strip Gatekeeper quarantine attributes from downloaded files.
MITRE ID: T1059.004 – Command and Scripting Interpreter: Unix Shell; T1059.002 – Command and Scripting Interpreter: AppleScript; T1218 – System Binary Proxy Execution; T1647 – Plist File Modification
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Gatekeeper and Notarization Bypass on macOS: Attackers abuse disk image mounting, zip slip extraction, or bundle directory structures to deliver payloads that macOS Gatekeeper fails to quarantine. Atomic Stealer (AMOS) and related infostealer families have consistently exploited this vector since 2023.
MITRE ID: T1553.001 – Subvert Trust Controls: Gatekeeper Bypass; T1204.002 – User Execution: Malicious File
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: TCC Abuse on macOS: Exploitation or social engineering of macOS Transparency, Consent and Control (TCC) permissions to grant malicious applications Full Disk Access, screen recording, camera, microphone, or contact access. TCC database manipulation via SQLite injection is a documented technique for bypassing user consent prompts without interaction.
MITRE ID: T1548 – Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism; T1056.001 – Input Capture: Keylogging
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Weak Application Control: Absence of enforced application allowlisting via Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) or AppLocker on Windows, or AMFI and Notarization enforcement on macOS, permits execution of untrusted or unsigned binaries and interpreted scripts without restriction. Excel 4.0 (XLM) macros remain an active bypass vector on Windows endpoints where macro policy is not fully enforced via Group Policy or Intune.
MITRE ID: T1553 – Subvert Trust Controls; T1036 – Masquerading
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Device" → "Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: EDR and XDR Agent Tampering: Adversaries actively target endpoint detection and response agents as a first post-compromise action. Techniques include kernel driver blocklist circumvention, safe-mode boot EDR bypass (agents do not load in Windows Safe Mode on most EDR platforms), process injection into EDR processes, and tamper-protection bypass via legitimate local admin credentials. Recorded in financial sector incidents attributed to ransomware groups and nation-state actors.
MITRE ID: T1562.001 – Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools; T1055 – Process Injection; T1014 – Rootkit
Change Log: WS2-B: Reclassified S2.1.3 → S2.1.8. EDR tampering is a post-exploitation memory/kernel technique (BYOVD driver loading, PPL bypass, HVCI defeat). Controls MEM-04 (HVCI + driver blocklist) and MEM-01 (EDR kernel telemetry) in Layer 8 are the correct remediation layer. OS & Kernel MD has no corresponding control.

Key Threats: Insufficient Audit Logging: Gaps in OS-level audit policy — Windows Security Event Log and Sysmon; macOS Unified Logging and OpenBSM audit — prevent detection of privilege escalation, persistence installation, credential access, and lateral movement originating from the endpoint. Fileless and living-off-the-land techniques are specifically designed to exploit telemetry blind spots in file-write-only detection pipelines.
MITRE ID: T1562.002 – Impair Defenses: Disable Windows Event Logging
Change Log: WS2-C: Reclassified S2.1.3 → S2.1.15. Audit log completeness and tamper-resistance are Management Plane governance controls. PE-05 (PIP data source completeness) and PE-06 (immutable WORM audit logging) in Layer 15 are the correct remediation controls. OS-level logging configuration is a contributing mechanism, not the governance layer.

Key Threats: Exposed Network Services: Open and listening services on the endpoint (RDP, SSH, SMB, WinRM on Windows; Remote Login SSH, Screen Sharing ARD/VNC, SMB on macOS) discoverable via network scanning and targeted for brute-force, credential spray, or direct exploitation when endpoints are not protected by host-based firewall policy.
MITRE ID: T1021 – Remote Services; T1133 – External Remote Services; T1110 – Brute Force
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Network; Device" → "Networks; Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Rogue Wireless Access Points and Evil Twin Attacks: Attackers deploy access points mimicking trusted corporate SSIDs to intercept credentials and session tokens from endpoints that auto-connect to previously seen networks. Particularly relevant for travelling employees in airports, hotels, and client sites.
MITRE ID: T1557 – Adversary-in-the-Middle; T1040 – Network Sniffing
Change Log: WS2-A: Reclassified S2.1.4 → S2.1.13. No controls exist for this threat in Layer 4 (Network MD). All controls (WL-01 WPA3-Enterprise, WL-02 MDM Wi-Fi profile, WL-03 WIDS, WL-08 ZTNA primary control) reside in Layer 13.

Key Threats: LLMNR / NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay: Link-Local Multicast Name Resolution and NetBIOS Name Service poisoning on Windows endpoints allows an attacker on the same network segment to capture NTLMv2 challenge-response hashes or relay credentials directly to other hosts. Remains an active and highly effective attack on insufficiently hardened Windows corporate LAN segments.
MITRE ID: T1557.001 – Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Network; Identity" → "Networks; Identity" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: VPN Split Tunnelling Exploitation: Misconfigured VPN split tunnelling routes corporate traffic securely while leaving internet-bound traffic unprotected and uninspected. Attackers exploit this to establish C2 channels, exfiltrate data, or pivot to cloud-hosted resources outside of corporate proxy and DLP visibility.
MITRE ID: T1572 – Protocol Tunneling; T1090 – Proxy
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Network" → "Networks" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: QUIC and HTTP/3 Inspection Gap: Corporate proxies and next-generation firewalls largely cannot inspect QUIC protocol traffic. Adversaries and malware families abuse QUIC to establish C2 channels and exfiltrate data outside of TLS inspection pipelines, creating a persistent blind spot in network telemetry.
MITRE ID: T1572 – Protocol Tunneling; T1071.001 – Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Network" → "Networks" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: IPv6 Lateral Movement and Rogue Router Advertisement: Endpoints with IPv6 enabled but without corresponding IPv6-aware network controls are susceptible to rogue Router Advertisement attacks that redirect traffic through an attacker-controlled gateway. Many corporate network monitoring tools remain IPv4-centric, leaving IPv6 traffic uninspected.
MITRE ID: T1557 – Adversary-in-the-Middle; T1210 – Exploitation of Remote Services
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Network" → "Networks" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: DNS Hijacking and DNS over HTTPS Bypass: Attackers modify local DNS resolution via host file manipulation, malicious DHCP responses, or malware-configured DoH resolvers to redirect endpoints to attacker-controlled infrastructure and bypass corporate DNS filtering and threat intelligence feeds.
MITRE ID: T1071.004 – Application Layer Protocol: DNS; T1565.001 – Data Manipulation: Stored Data Manipulation
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Network" → "Networks" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Cellular Interface Breakout (eSIM and Physical SIM): Endpoints equipped with integrated cellular modems — via eSIM or physical SIM — can bypass all corporate network controls, proxy enforcement, and DLP inspection by routing traffic directly over carrier networks. Both eSIM and physical SIM configurations present equivalent risk and should be subject to equivalent MDM policy enforcement.
MITRE ID: T1572 – Protocol Tunneling; T1048 – Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
Change Log: WS3-A: Threat retained S2.1.4. NEW CONTROL REQUIRED: NC-14 (Cellular/eSIM MDM governance) to be authored in Layer 4 MD. Controls: MDM-enforced per-app VPN on cellular, eSIM provisioning via ABM/Intune, ZTNA tunnel mandatory on cellular data.

Key Threats: Malicious Office Macros and Document-Borne Execution: Macro-enabled Office documents delivered via email or web download execute VBA or Excel 4.0 (XLM) macros to download and run malicious payloads. Microsoft disabled VBA macros from internet-sourced files by default from 2022; however, Excel 4.0 XLM macros remain a viable bypass vector on endpoints where macro policy is not comprehensively enforced via Group Policy or Intune configuration profiles.
MITRE ID: T1566.001 – Phishing: Spearphishing Attachment; T1059.005 – Command and Scripting Interpreter: Visual Basic; T1204.002 – User Execution: Malicious File

Key Threats: Unpatched Third-Party Applications: Widely deployed applications including browsers, PDF readers, collaboration tools, and financial data terminals (e.g., Bloomberg, Reuters Eikon) running outdated versions expose known CVEs exploitable via drive-by download or malicious document rendering. Third-party applications frequently lag behind OS-level patch cycles and may not be covered by centralised patch management tooling.
MITRE ID: T1203 – Exploitation for Client Execution; T1068 – Exploitation for Privilege Escalation

Key Threats: Browser-Based Attacks and Malicious Extensions: Credential theft, session hijacking, and data exfiltration via malicious or compromised browser extensions, drive-by downloads, and adversarial JavaScript. Browser extensions operate with broad permissions and are not subject to the same application control policies as native executables on most endpoint configurations.
MITRE ID: T1185 – Browser Session Hijacking; T1176 – Browser Extensions; T1059.007 – Command and Scripting Interpreter: JavaScript

Key Threats: Electron Application Vulnerabilities: Widely deployed enterprise applications built on the Electron framework — including Microsoft Teams, Slack, Visual Studio Code, and Bloomberg Terminal — expose a Node.js runtime within the application boundary. Misconfigured nodeIntegration or contextIsolation bypass vulnerabilities allow remote code execution within the application context, potentially escalating to OS-level access.
MITRE ID: T1203 – Exploitation for Client Execution; T1059.007 – Command and Scripting Interpreter: JavaScript

Key Threats: Software Supply Chain Compromise: Malicious code injected into legitimate software build pipelines or package repositories (npm, PyPI) results in trojanised application installers or libraries deployed to endpoints via standard software distribution channels. Relevant to developer endpoints and financial platforms with scripting dependencies.
MITRE ID: T1195.001 – Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools; T1195.002 – Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain

Key Threats: Malicious VS Code Extensions: The Visual Studio Code marketplace does not enforce the same security review standards as OS-level application stores. Malicious or compromised extensions with access to the filesystem, terminal, and network can exfiltrate source code, credentials, and secrets from developer and DevOps endpoints within the bank's engineering function.
MITRE ID: T1195.001 – Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools; T1552.001 – Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files

Key Threats: WDAC and AppLocker Bypass via MSIX/AppX Packages: Attackers package malicious payloads as MSIX or AppX application bundles to bypass Windows Defender Application Control and AppLocker policies that do not explicitly account for packaged application trust rules. An active living-off-the-land gap on Windows 10 and Windows 11 endpoints where application control policy has not been updated to address this trust path.
MITRE ID: T1553 – Subvert Trust Controls; T1036 – Masquerading

Key Threats: Infostealer Malware Targeting Endpoint Credentials: Commodity and MaaS infostealer families including LummaC2, Atomic Stealer (AMOS), Rhadamanthys, and Vidar systematically harvest browser-saved credentials, session cookies, crypto wallets, and application tokens from both Windows and macOS endpoints. Infostealers are delivered via malvertising, trojanised installers, and phishing and frequently evade signature-based detection at point of execution.
MITRE ID: T1555 – Credentials from Password Stores; T1539 – Steal Web Session Cookie; T1005 – Data from Local System

Key Threats: Insecure Application Data Storage: Applications storing sensitive data — credentials, session tokens, API keys, PII — in plaintext within local configuration files, SQLite databases, application logs, or Keychain entries accessible to other processes. Particularly relevant for financial data terminal client applications and internally developed tooling deployed to the corporate laptop estate.
MITRE ID: T1552.001 – Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files; T1005 – Data from Local System

Key Threats: AiTM Phishing Proxies: Real-time phishing proxy toolkits (Evilginx3, Modlishka, Muraena) intercept MFA codes and session cookies mid-authentication, bypassing TOTP and push-based MFA entirely. Phishing-resistant authentication via FIDO2 hardware security keys or Windows Hello for Business is the only control that reliably defeats this attack class.
MITRE ID: T1566.002 – Phishing: Spearphishing Link; T1557 – Adversary-in-the-Middle; T1539 – Steal Web Session Cookie

Key Threats: Primary Refresh Token (PRT) Theft: The Primary Refresh Token is a durable SSO credential issued to Entra ID joined and hybrid-joined Windows endpoints, stored and protected by the TPM where available. Theft via LSASS access on endpoints without TPM-bound PRT protection allows an attacker to authenticate to any Entra ID resource, generate new access tokens, and bypass Conditional Access policies without re-authentication.
MITRE ID: T1528 – Steal Application Access Token; T1550.004 – Use Alternate Authentication Material: Web Session Cookie

Key Threats: Pass-the-Hash and Pass-the-Ticket: Extracted NTLM hashes or Kerberos tickets used for lateral movement without knowledge of plaintext credentials. Credential Guard on Windows 11 isolates LSA secrets within a VBS enclave, materially reducing but not eliminating this attack surface. Hybrid-joined endpoints may expose both Kerberos and cloud token material simultaneously.
MITRE ID: T1550.002 – Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash; T1550.003 – Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Ticket
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Identity; Device" → "Identity; Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Kerberoasting and AS-REP Roasting: Service account Kerberos tickets requested and cracked offline to recover plaintext service account passwords. Root cause is service accounts with weak passwords and excessive privilege. Maps to the Governance cross-cutting capability in CISA ZTMM v2 — service account lifecycle and privilege governance is the primary preventive control.
MITRE ID: T1558.003 – Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Kerberoasting; T1558.004 – Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: AS-REP Roasting

Key Threats: MFA Fatigue and Push Notification Bombing: Adversaries generate repeated MFA push notifications to enrolled users until the user approves an illegitimate request out of fatigue or confusion. Documented in multiple financial sector incidents. Mitigated by number matching, additional context in push notifications, and phishing-resistant MFA enforcement via Conditional Access policy.
MITRE ID: T1621 – Multi-Factor Authentication Request Generation

Key Threats: Windows Hello for Business Misconfiguration: WHfB deployments using certificate trust model versus cloud Kerberos trust model present materially different attack surfaces. Certificate trust deployments are susceptible to Active Directory Certificate Services (ADCS) abuse (ESC1-ESC8 misconfigurations) enabling privilege escalation and domain compromise via certificate template manipulation.
MITRE ID: T1649 – Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates; T1068 – Exploitation for Privilege Escalation

Key Threats: FIDO2 and Passkey Credential Risks: Hardware-bound FIDO2 security keys (YubiKey, FIDO2 tokens) are not syncable and are resistant to phishing and AiTM attacks. Device-bound synced passkeys stored in platform authenticators (iCloud Keychain, Windows Hello) are syncable across devices and are recoverable via cloud account compromise. These two credential classes present materially different risk profiles and should be distinguished in MFA policy design.
MITRE ID: T1556 – Modify Authentication Process; T1528 – Steal Application Access Token

Key Threats: Continuous Access Evaluation (CAE) Propagation Latency: CAE-capable clients receive token revocation signals within approximately 15 minutes for most revocation events under Microsoft's implementation. Critical events such as password change and explicit admin revocation approach near-real-time revocation. This latency window represents a residual risk interval during which a compromised session token remains valid and should be factored into incident response playbooks.
MITRE ID: T1550.001 – Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token

Key Threats: Cross-Tenant B2B Token Abuse: Guest accounts provisioned in partner or vendor Entra ID tenants via B2B collaboration with excessive permissions or absence of guest access controls enable lateral movement between tenant boundaries. Particularly relevant during M&A integration periods where trust relationships between tenants are established without full security review.
MITRE ID: T1550.001 – Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token; T1078 – Valid Accounts

Key Threats: Removable Media Exfiltration: Sensitive files, customer PII, trade data, and credentials copied to unmanaged USB storage devices or external drives in the absence of enforced removable media controls via MDM policy or endpoint DLP agents. Applicable to both Windows and macOS endpoints without device control policy enforcement.
MITRE ID: T1052.001 – Exfiltration Over Physical Medium: Exfiltration over USB; T1005 – Data from Local System

Key Threats: Cloud Storage and Personal SaaS Upload: Sensitive corporate data uploaded to personal cloud storage (Google Drive, Dropbox, iCloud, OneDrive personal) or consumer SaaS platforms outside of corporate visibility and DLP inspection. Inline CASB enforcement is required to detect and block this channel; endpoint DLP agent coverage alone is insufficient for browser-based uploads on managed devices.
MITRE ID: T1567.002 – Exfiltration Over Web Service: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage

Key Threats: Print-to-PDF and Physical Printing as DLP Bypass: Converting sensitive documents to PDF via the OS print subsystem or printing physically bypasses content-inspection DLP controls on most endpoint DLP deployments. Neither Windows nor macOS print pipeline activity is natively intercepted by standard DLP agent implementations without specific print control policy enforcement.
MITRE ID: T1052 – Exfiltration Over Physical Medium; T1005 – Data from Local System

Key Threats: OCR-Based Exfiltration Circumvention: Adversaries and malicious insiders convert sensitive documents or screen content to images prior to upload, circumventing content-inspection DLP engines that rely on text extraction for classification. As of 2025-2026, this technique is increasingly accessible via AI-assisted tooling available on the endpoint.
MITRE ID: T1027 – Obfuscated Files or Information; T1048 – Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol

Key Threats: Generative AI and LLM Data Submission: Employees submitting sensitive customer data, internal financial models, source code, or M&A information to consumer-facing generative AI platforms (ChatGPT, Google Gemini, Microsoft Copilot consumer tier). Data submitted to consumer-tier AI services is subject to provider data retention policies that may include use for model training. Enterprise-tier services with data processing agreements present a materially different risk profile and should be distinguished in acceptable use policy.
MITRE ID: T1567 – Exfiltration Over Web Service; T1213 – Data from Information Repositories

Key Threats: Endpoint DLP Agent Bypass via Virtualisation or Dual Boot: Running an unmanaged virtual machine or booting to an alternate OS from external media on a corporate endpoint creates an execution environment outside the coverage of the installed DLP agent, EDR, and MDM controls. Data accessible on the host filesystem may be reachable from the guest environment depending on virtualisation configuration.
MITRE ID: T1564.006 – Hide Artifacts: Run Virtual Instance; T1006 – Direct Volume Access

Key Threats: Sensitive Data in Unmanaged Application Logs and Temp Files: Application diagnostic logs, crash dumps, browser cache, and operating system temporary file locations may contain credentials, session tokens, PII, or financial data written during normal application operation outside of the scope of DLP content inspection policies.
MITRE ID: T1005 – Data from Local System; T1552.001 – Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files

Key Threats: Encrypted Exfiltration Channel Bypassing DLP Inspection: Data exfiltrated via end-to-end encrypted channels — including DNS over HTTPS, QUIC, or encrypted messaging applications — evades DLP inspection engines that rely on TLS break-and-inspect. Without full SSL inspection coverage and protocol-level enforcement, encrypted exfiltration channels represent a structural gap in DLP architecture.
MITRE ID: T1048.002 – Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol: Exfiltration Over Asymmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol; T1572 – Protocol Tunneling
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Data; Network" → "Data; Networks" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Process Injection: Malicious code injected into the memory space of legitimate running processes (svchost.exe, explorer.exe, lsass.exe on Windows; launchd, coreaudiod on macOS) to execute without writing payloads to disk and to inherit the privileges and trust context of the host process. Commonly used post-exploitation by commodity malware, ransomware affiliates, and nation-state tooling.
MITRE ID: T1055 – Process Injection; T1055.001 – Process Injection: Dynamic-link Library Injection; T1055.012 – Process Injection: Process Hollowing

Key Threats: Reflective DLL Injection and In-Memory PE Loading: Malicious DLLs or portable executables loaded directly into process memory without touching the filesystem, bypassing file-based AV scanning, application control hash verification, and most EDR file-write telemetry pipelines. Widely used in post-exploitation frameworks including Cobalt Strike, Brute Ratel, and Havoc.
MITRE ID: T1055.001 – Process Injection: Dynamic-link Library Injection; T1620 – Reflective Code Loading

Key Threats: Asynchronous Procedure Call and ListPlanting Injection: APC injection queues malicious code execution into threads of targeted legitimate processes via the Windows APC mechanism, evading many injection detection heuristics that focus on CreateRemoteThread-based patterns. ListPlanting (T1055.015) abuses Windows list-view controls to redirect execution flow without conventional injection APIs, a technique actively used to evade modern EDR behavioural detection.
MITRE ID: T1055.004 – Process Injection: Asynchronous Procedure Call; T1055.015 – Process Injection: ListPlanting

Key Threats: Phantom DLL Hijacking: Malicious DLLs placed at filesystem paths referenced in the Windows registry or application manifests but where no legitimate DLL exists, causing the OS loader to load the attacker-supplied binary with the privileges of the loading process. Distinct from standard DLL search order hijacking and not addressed by controls that focus solely on PATH-based search order manipulation.
MITRE ID: T1574.001 – Hijack Execution Flow: DLL Search Order Hijacking; T1574.002 – Hijack Execution Flow: DLL Side-Loading

Key Threats: PowerShell and .NET In-Memory Execution: PowerShell AMSI bypass techniques combined with .NET assembly loading via [System.Reflection.Assembly]::Load() allow execution of arbitrary managed code entirely in memory without disk writes. Widely used in both commodity and targeted attack chains against Windows endpoints where script block logging and AMSI are not enforced via Group Policy or Intune.
MITRE ID: T1059.001 – Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell; T1620 – Reflective Code Loading; T1562.001 – Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

Key Threats: LSASS Memory Credential Harvesting: Direct or indirect access to the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service process memory to extract plaintext credentials, NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and DPAPI masterkeys. Mitigated by Credential Guard on VBS-enabled Windows 11 endpoints and by PPL (Protected Process Light) for LSASS; however, BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) techniques are actively used to disable these protections.
MITRE ID: T1003.001 – OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory; T1068 – Exploitation for Privilege Escalation

Key Threats: Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver (BYOVD): Adversaries load legitimately signed but vulnerable third-party kernel drivers to achieve kernel-level code execution, disable EDR and security controls, or tamper with Windows security features including PPL and VBS. BYOVD has been observed in ransomware campaigns and nation-state tooling targeting financial sector endpoints. Microsoft's Vulnerable Driver Blocklist requires active maintenance and enforcement via WDAC policy.
MITRE ID: T1068 – Exploitation for Privilege Escalation; T1014 – Rootkit; T1562.001 – Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

Key Threats: Heap Spray and Use-After-Free Exploitation: Memory corruption vulnerabilities in browsers, document rendering engines, and productivity applications exploited via crafted content to achieve arbitrary code execution within the application sandbox. Used as a first-stage exploit in browser-based drive-by download chains targeting financial sector endpoints via malvertising and spearphishing.
MITRE ID: T1203 – Exploitation for Client Execution; T1189 – Drive-by Compromise

Key Threats: Fileless Persistence via Registry and Scheduled Tasks: Malicious payloads encoded and stored within the Windows registry, scheduled tasks, or WMI subscriptions to achieve persistence without writing executable files to disk. Execution is triggered by system events, logon events, or time-based schedules, loading the payload directly into memory via PowerShell or wscript.
MITRE ID: T1547.001 – Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Registry Run Keys; T1053.005 – Scheduled Task/Job: Scheduled Task; T1546.003 – Event Triggered Execution: Windows Management Instrumentation Event Subscription

Key Threats: Entra ID Service Principal Credential Exposure: Client secrets and certificates for Entra ID application registrations stored on developer, DevOps, and administrator endpoints represent high-value targets granting tenant-wide API access. Compromise of a service principal with Microsoft Graph API permissions can expose all mailboxes, SharePoint sites, Teams channels, and Entra ID directory objects within the tenant without triggering user-centric Conditional Access policies.
MITRE ID: T1552.001 – Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files; T1528 – Steal Application Access Token

Key Threats: OAuth Token Theft and Illicit Consent Grant: Malicious OAuth applications registered in Entra ID solicit user consent to broad Graph API delegated permissions (Mail.Read, Files.ReadWrite.All, Contacts.Read). Once consented, the application retains persistent access independent of the user's password or MFA state. Illicit consent grant attacks are documented against financial sector M365 tenants and bypass conventional credential-based detection.
MITRE ID: T1528 – Steal Application Access Token; T1550.001 – Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token

Key Threats: Conditional Access Named Location Bypass: Attackers route authentication attempts through IP addresses within trusted Named Locations defined in Entra ID Conditional Access policy — including corporate VPN exit nodes, leased data centre ranges, or compromised on-premises infrastructure — to satisfy location-based policy conditions and suppress MFA requirements or access restrictions.
MITRE ID: T1078 – Valid Accounts; T1090 – Proxy
Change Log: WS1-A (residual): "Identity; Network" → "Identity; Networks"

Key Threats: Microsoft 365 Copilot and Copilot Agent Data Oversharing: Microsoft 365 Copilot operates on the authenticated user's effective permissions across the M365 estate. Users with access to overly permissive SharePoint sites, shared mailboxes, or Teams channels can inadvertently surface sensitive data — including M&A documents, HR records, and credit risk models — via natural language queries. Microsoft 365 Copilot agents configured with delegated Graph API permissions compound this risk by enabling automated data access at scale.
MITRE ID: T1213 – Data from Information Repositories; T1530 – Data from Cloud Storage
Change Log: WS3-B: Threat retained S2.1.9. NEW CONTROL REQUIRED: CS-12 (M365 Copilot data governance) to be authored in Layer 9 MD. Controls: SharePoint oversharing remediation prerequisite, sensitivity label filtering, Copilot interaction logging, agent creation governance.

Key Threats: Cloud Storage Misconfiguration and Oversharing: SharePoint Online sites, OneDrive folders, Teams channels, and Azure Blob Storage containers configured with overly permissive sharing settings expose sensitive data to authenticated-but-unauthorised internal users or, in misconfiguration cases, unauthenticated external access. Misconfigured sharing policies are a consistent finding in financial sector M365 security assessments.
MITRE ID: T1530 – Data from Cloud Storage; T1213.002 – Data from Information Repositories: SharePoint

Key Threats: SaaS Session Cookie Hijacking: Session cookies for cloud-hosted SaaS platforms (Salesforce, ServiceNow, Workday, M365) harvested from endpoint browsers via infostealer malware or AiTM phishing proxies and replayed from attacker-controlled infrastructure. Cookie replay bypasses MFA and Conditional Access controls where token binding is not enforced and CAE is not implemented by the SaaS provider.
MITRE ID: T1539 – Steal Web Session Cookie; T1550.004 – Use Alternate Authentication Material: Web Session Cookie

Key Threats: Misconfigured CASB and Cloud App Policy Gaps: Incomplete Cloud Access Security Broker policy coverage resulting in unsanctioned SaaS application usage, shadow IT data flows, and unmonitored API integrations between corporate and external cloud platforms. CASB inline mode is required for enforcement; API-mode-only deployments provide visibility without blocking capability and should not be treated as equivalent controls.
MITRE ID: T1567 – Exfiltration Over Web Service; T1048 – Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Applications & Workloads; Data; Network" → "Applications & Workloads; Data; Networks" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: GitHub Actions and CI/CD Secret Exfiltration: Repository secrets, GITHUB_TOKEN values, and environment variables accessible during CI/CD workflow runs on self-hosted runners operating on corporate endpoints. Compromise of a workflow file via pull request injection or repository misconfiguration can exfiltrate secrets to attacker-controlled infrastructure, with blast radius extending to the corporate endpoint and cloud environment if the runner holds cloud provider credentials.
MITRE ID: T1552.001 – Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files; T1195.002 – Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain

Key Threats: Insecure API Key and Secret Management on Endpoints: Cloud provider API keys (AWS, Azure, GCP), database connection strings, and third-party service credentials hardcoded in application configuration files, environment variable files (.env), shell profiles, or source code repositories on developer endpoints. Exposed secrets provide direct access to cloud resources and data stores outside of identity-based access controls.
MITRE ID: T1552.001 – Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files; T1552.004 – Unsecured Credentials: Private Keys

Key Threats: Spearphishing with Malicious Attachments: Targeted email-delivered payloads crafted to appear as legitimate financial documents, regulatory correspondence, or internal communications. Malicious Office documents, PDFs, ISO/IMG disk images, and LNK files remain primary delivery mechanisms. ISO and IMG container files were widely adopted following Microsoft's 2022 macro blocking policy as they bypass Mark-of-the-Web propagation to enclosed files on Windows.
MITRE ID: T1566.001 – Phishing: Spearphishing Attachment; T1204.002 – User Execution: Malicious File

Key Threats: Spearphishing via Malicious Links and AiTM Proxy Pages: Targeted emails delivering links to credential harvesting pages or AiTM phishing proxy infrastructure (Evilginx3, Tycoon 2FA, Caffeine PhaaS). Tycoon 2FA and similar Phishing-as-a-Service platforms specifically target Microsoft 365 and enable real-time session cookie interception at scale, lowering the technical barrier for financial sector targeting significantly.
MITRE ID: T1566.002 – Phishing: Spearphishing Link; T1557 – Adversary-in-the-Middle

Key Threats: Microsoft Teams External Tenant Phishing: Attackers with any Microsoft 365 tenant — including free or trial tenants — send malicious files, links, or social engineering messages to corporate Teams users via external federated messaging. This channel bypasses email gateway and secure email gateway controls entirely and is not subject to the same attachment scanning and link detonation policies applied to SMTP-delivered email.
MITRE ID: T1566.002 – Phishing: Spearphishing Link; T1534 – Internal Spearphishing

Key Threats: Business Email Compromise and CEO Fraud: Adversaries compromise or spoof executive email accounts to authorise fraudulent wire transfers, redirect payroll, or manipulate financial operations. BEC remains one of the highest-financial-impact attack categories for financial institutions globally per FBI IC3 reporting. DMARC enforcement, DKIM signing, and out-of-band verification procedures are primary preventive controls.
MITRE ID: T1586.002 – Compromise Accounts: Email Accounts; T1656 – Impersonation

Key Threats: AI-Generated Deepfake Audio and Video in Communication Channels: Synthetic voice and video content generated using publicly available AI tooling used to impersonate senior executives, relationship managers, or regulators in video conferences, voice calls, or recorded messages to authorise fraudulent transactions or data disclosures. The 2024 Hong Kong incident — in which a finance employee was deceived via a deepfake video conference call resulting in a USD 25 million fraudulent transfer — represents a confirmed financial sector precedent.
MITRE ID: T1036.001 – Masquerading: Invalid Code Signature; T1598 – Phishing for Information; T1656 – Impersonation

Key Threats: Email Rule and Forwarding Abuse Post-Compromise: Following mailbox compromise, adversaries create hidden inbox rules to forward copies of email to external addresses, delete security alerts, or suppress MFA and password reset notifications. This technique maintains persistent intelligence access while degrading the victim's awareness of the compromise. Detectable via M365 audit log monitoring for New-InboxRule and Set-InboxRule events.
MITRE ID: T1114.003 – Email Collection: Email Forwarding Rule; T1564.008 – Hide Artifacts: Email Hiding Rules

Key Threats: Homoglyph and Unicode Domain Spoofing: Phishing domains constructed using visually identical Unicode characters (Cyrillic, Greek, Latin look-alikes) to impersonate legitimate corporate or counterparty domains in email addresses, hyperlinks, and document content. Effective against users performing visual inspection of URLs and against email gateway domain reputation checks that do not implement homoglyph detection.
MITRE ID: T1036.005 – Masquerading: Match Legitimate Name or Location; T1566.002 – Phishing: Spearphishing Link

Key Threats: Collaboration Platform Data Exfiltration: Sensitive data shared via Microsoft Teams, Slack, Zoom chat, or Bloomberg Messaging to personal accounts, external guests, or unauthorised channels outside of DLP and data governance policy coverage. Collaboration platforms represent a significant and frequently under-monitored data exfiltration channel, particularly where DLP policy has not been extended beyond M365 email to cover Teams and SharePoint content.
MITRE ID: T1048 – Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol; T1213 – Data from Information Repositories

Key Threats: Trojanised Software Installer Delivery: Malicious code injected into legitimate software build pipelines or distributed via compromised vendor update mechanisms to deliver backdoored application versions to endpoints through trusted distribution channels. SolarWinds SUNBURST and 3CX Desktop App supply chain compromises are confirmed financial sector precedents demonstrating the viability and impact of this vector.
MITRE ID: T1195.002 – Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain; T1072 – Software Deployment Tools

Key Threats: Remote Monitoring and Management Tool Abuse: Legitimate RMM platforms (ConnectWise ScreenConnect, AnyDesk, TeamViewer, Atera) used by managed service providers and IT support vendors abused by adversaries to establish persistent remote access to endpoints. ConnectWise ScreenConnect CVE-2024-1708 and CVE-2024-1709 (unauthenticated remote code execution, CVSS 10.0) were actively exploited in 2024 with confirmed impact on financial sector endpoints. RMM tools frequently operate outside of application control and EDR behavioural policy due to legacy vendor exceptions.
MITRE ID: T1219 – Remote Access Software; T1133 – External Remote Services
Change Log: WS3-F: Threat retained S2.1.11. NEW CONTROL REQUIRED: SC-11 (RMM tool abuse prevention) to be authored in Layer 11 MD. Controls: WDAC allowlist blocks unauthorised RMM binaries, SWG category block for RMM relay infrastructure, EDR process behaviour alerting for attacker-deployed RMM C2.

Key Threats: Malicious or Compromised npm and PyPI Packages: Open-source package dependencies introduced into internally developed applications or developer toolchains via typosquatting, dependency confusion, or direct package repository compromise. Malicious packages executing at install time or at runtime on developer endpoints can exfiltrate credentials, establish reverse shells, or modify locally built artefacts prior to deployment.
MITRE ID: T1195.001 – Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools; T1059 – Command and Scripting Interpreter

Key Threats: Malicious Visual Studio Code Extensions: The VS Code marketplace does not enforce equivalent security review standards to OS-level application stores. Malicious or compromised extensions operating within the VS Code runtime have access to the filesystem, integrated terminal, environment variables, and network on developer and DevOps endpoints. Extensions impersonating popular legitimate extensions via typosquatting have been documented targeting engineering teams in financial institutions.
MITRE ID: T1195.001 – Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools; T1552.001 – Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files

Key Threats: Third-Party Vendor Endpoint Access Abuse: Vendors, system integrators, and outsourced support providers with privileged access to the bank’s endpoint estate via VPN, jump hosts, or direct RMM connectivity represent a high-risk access pathway. Compromise of a vendor endpoint or vendor credentials provides an attacker with access equivalent to the vendor’s permissions without triggering endpoint-sourced anomaly detection on bank-managed devices.
MITRE ID: T1078.004 – Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts; T1199 – Trusted Relationship
Change Log: WS1-A (residual): "Identity; Devices; Network" → "Identity; Devices; Networks"

Key Threats: Hardware Supply Chain Implants: Malicious hardware components or firmware modifications introduced during device manufacturing, logistics, or distribution prior to endpoint deployment. Relevant for custom device configurations procured through non-standard supply chains or sourced from third-party refurbishers outside of OEM-certified channels. Presents a near-zero detection probability using software-only controls.
MITRE ID: T1195.003 – Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Hardware Supply Chain; T1542.001 – Pre-OS Boot: System Firmware

Key Threats: CI/CD Pipeline Compromise and Build Environment Injection: Malicious code injected into CI/CD pipeline configurations (GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps, Jenkins) via compromised pipeline credentials, pull request injection, or poisoned pipeline execution (PPE) techniques. Self-hosted runners operating on corporate endpoints extend the blast radius of pipeline compromise to the endpoint estate and any cloud credentials accessible from the runner environment.
MITRE ID: T1195.002 – Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain; T1552.001 – Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files

Key Threats: SBOM Gaps and Untracked Dependency Risk: Absence of a maintained Software Bill of Materials for internally developed and vendor-supplied applications prevents timely identification of vulnerable or compromised components following disclosure of new CVEs or supply chain incidents. Without SBOM coverage, the bank cannot reliably determine exposure to events such as Log4Shell within the required regulatory notification timeframes applicable to tier-1 financial institutions.
MITRE ID: T1195.001 – Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools

Key Threats: Managed Service Provider Lateral Movement: MSP-managed endpoints sharing common tooling, credentials, or network access pathways across multiple client environments create cross-client lateral movement risk. Compromise of MSP management infrastructure provides an attacker with simultaneous access to all managed client environments, as demonstrated in the Kaseya VSA ransomware incident affecting downstream clients across multiple sectors.
MITRE ID: T1199 – Trusted Relationship; T1021 – Remote Services; T1078 – Valid Accounts
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Devices; Network; Identity" → "Devices; Networks; Identity" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Malicious Insider Data Exfiltration: Employees, contractors, or privileged users intentionally exfiltrating sensitive data — customer PII, trading positions, M&A intelligence, source code, or credit risk models — via removable media, personal cloud storage, personal email, or collaboration platforms in advance of resignation, termination, or in response to external solicitation. Insider threat incidents in financial institutions frequently involve bulk data staging over an extended period prior to the exfiltration event.
MITRE ID: T1052 – Exfiltration Over Physical Medium; T1567 – Exfiltration Over Web Service; T1005 – Data from Local System

Key Threats: Privilege Abuse by Administrator and Privileged Accounts: IT administrators, cloud platform engineers, database administrators, and security operations personnel abusing elevated access rights to access data beyond their authorised scope, disable security controls, create backdoor accounts, or modify audit logging configuration to suppress evidence of activity. Privileged access without just-in-time scoping, session recording, and independent audit represents a structural Zero-Trust gap.
MITRE ID: T1078.003 – Valid Accounts: Local Accounts; T1562.002 – Impair Defenses: Disable Windows Event Logging; T1136 – Create Account

Key Threats: Negligent Insider and Accidental Data Disclosure: Unintentional data exposure resulting from misdirected email, misconfigured sharing permissions, accidental upload to public repositories, or erroneous responses to social engineering. Negligent insider incidents constitute a significant proportion of data loss events in financial institutions and are not addressed by threat-focused controls alone — data classification, user awareness, and friction-based DLP controls are the primary mitigations.
MITRE ID: T1048 – Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol; T1213 – Data from Information Repositories

Key Threats: Bring-Your-Own-Identity Risk: Employees authenticating to corporate SaaS platforms using personal identity providers federated via Entra ID external identities or social login integrations. Personal identity accounts are outside corporate password policy, MFA enforcement, and account lifecycle governance, creating an unmanaged authentication pathway to corporate resources that persists beyond employment termination if not explicitly deprovisioned.
MITRE ID: T1078 – Valid Accounts; T1556 – Modify Authentication Process

Key Threats: Shadow IT and Unsanctioned Application Usage: Employees deploying unsanctioned SaaS applications, browser extensions, or locally installed tools to process corporate data outside of approved application governance and DLP policy coverage. Shadow IT creates unmonitored data flows and introduces unvetted third-party data processors into the bank's data handling chain without formal risk assessment or contractual data protection controls.
MITRE ID: T1567 – Exfiltration Over Web Service; T1213 – Data from Information Repositories

Key Threats: UEBA Evasion and Low-and-Slow Exfiltration: Adversaries and malicious insiders deliberately operating below User and Entity Behaviour Analytics detection thresholds by staging and exfiltrating data in low-volume increments over extended periods, using normal working hours and approved applications to blend activity with legitimate user behaviour. UEBA baseline periods and alert thresholds must be tuned specifically for financial sector data sensitivity and role-based access patterns.
MITRE ID: T1029 – Scheduled Transfer; T1030 – Data Transfer Size Limits

Key Threats: Insider Threat Facilitation of External Attack: Employees or contractors knowingly providing credentials, access tokens, internal network information, or security control configurations to external threat actors in exchange for financial compensation. Documented in ransomware affiliate recruitment campaigns specifically targeting financial sector employees via dark web forums and encrypted messaging platforms as of 2024-2025.
MITRE ID: T1078 – Valid Accounts; T1199 – Trusted Relationship

Key Threats: Offboarding Access Retention: Failure to fully deprovision access for departing employees across all systems — including legacy applications, shared service accounts, VPN certificates, and SaaS platforms not integrated with the identity governance platform — leaves active access pathways that may be exploited post-departure. Entra ID account disable does not automatically revoke active sessions, PRT tokens, or access in federated applications without CAE enforcement.
MITRE ID: T1078 – Valid Accounts; T1550.001 – Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token

Key Threats: Evil Twin and Rogue Access Point Credential Interception: Attacker-controlled wireless access points mimicking trusted corporate or public SSIDs capture credentials and session tokens from endpoints that auto-connect to previously joined networks. WPA2-Enterprise with certificate-based mutual authentication is required to prevent rogue AP association; PSK-based networks remain vulnerable to this attack class regardless of passphrase complexity.
MITRE ID: T1557 – Adversary-in-the-Middle; T1040 – Network Sniffing
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Network" → "Networks" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: WPA3 SAE Dragonblood-Class Side-Channel Attacks: The WPA3 Simultaneous Authentication of Equals handshake is susceptible to timing and cache-based side-channel attacks (Dragonblood, 2019 and subsequent research) that enable offline dictionary attacks against WPA3-Personal networks. Relevant for corporate wireless infrastructure migrating to WPA3 without validation of access point firmware patches addressing SAE implementation vulnerabilities.
MITRE ID: T1557 – Adversary-in-the-Middle; T1040 – Network Sniffing
Change Log: WS3-H: Threat retained S2.1.13. EXISTING CONTROL WL-01 ADEQUATE — rationale note required. WPA3-Enterprise with EAP-TLS does not use SAE handshake; Dragonblood-class side-channel attacks target WPA3-Personal SAE only. WL-01 architect note to be added to Layer 13 MD confirming inherent immunity.

Key Threats: Bluetooth Exploitation – BrakTooth and BLUFFS: Identification of a class of vulnerabilities in commercial Bluetooth Classic stacks enabling denial of service and arbitrary code execution on affected chipsets. BLUFFS (2023) demonstrated Bluetooth session key derivation weaknesses enabling session impersonation and MitM attacks across Bluetooth BR/EDR connections. These represent the current applicable Bluetooth vulnerability classes for 2025-2026 and supersede the BlueBorne (2017) class of vulnerabilities which are patched on supported OS versions.
MITRE ID: T1011.001 – Exfiltration Over Bluetooth; T1557 – Adversary-in-the-Middle
Change Log: WS3-G: Threat retained S2.1.13. NEW CONTROL REQUIRED: WL-09 (Bluetooth Classic protocol hardening) to be authored in Layer 13 MD. Controls: BT Classic (BR/EDR) disable where only BLE in use, firmware update cadence for BT chipsets, MDM-enforced BT version/capability restriction.

Key Threats: Near-Field Communication Relay and Eavesdropping: NFC relay attacks using commodity Android tooling (NFCGate) intercept and replay NFC communications from corporate access cards or payment-enabled devices within proximity, potentially enabling physical access bypass or contactless payment fraud. Relevant for endpoints with NFC capabilities used in conjunction with corporate identity and access management.
MITRE ID: T1200 – Hardware Additions; T1557 – Adversary-in-the-Middle
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Devices; Network" → "Devices; Networks" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Captive Portal Credential Harvesting: Airport, hotel, and conference venue captive portals serving credential phishing pages — including convincing replicas of corporate SSO login pages — or performing SSL stripping prior to granting internet access. Particularly high risk for travelling employees operating outside corporate network controls without enforced always-on VPN or Zero-Trust Network Access (ZTNA) policy.
MITRE ID: T1557 – Adversary-in-the-Middle; T1056.003 – Input Capture: Web Portal Capture
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Network; Identity" → "Networks; Identity" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Wi-Fi Auto-Connect and Preferred Network List Abuse: Endpoints configured to auto-connect to previously joined SSIDs are susceptible to rogue AP association in any location where an attacker can broadcast a matching SSID. Corporate MDM policy should enforce disabling auto-join for non-corporate SSIDs and clearing Preferred Network List entries for generic public SSIDs (e.g., "Airport_WiFi", "Starbucks") that are trivially spoofable.
MITRE ID: T1557 – Adversary-in-the-Middle; T1040 – Network Sniffing
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Network; Devices" → "Networks; Devices" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: Wireless Deauthentication and Denial of Service: IEEE 802.11 management frame spoofing used to force endpoint deauthentication from corporate wireless infrastructure, compelling reconnection to an attacker-controlled evil twin AP or disrupting endpoint connectivity to force failover to cellular or untrusted networks outside corporate traffic inspection. 802.11w management frame protection mitigates this attack and should be enforced on corporate wireless infrastructure.
MITRE ID: T1498 – Network Denial of Service; T1557 – Adversary-in-the-Middle
Change Log: WS1-A: Pillar normalised "Network" → "Networks" per CISA ZT Maturity Model v2 controlled vocabulary

Key Threats: [PHYSICAL SECURITY PROGRAMME SCOPE — No Technical ZTA Control] Proximity-Based Social Engineering and Physical Tailgating: Attackers gaining physical proximity to endpoints and employees in corporate premises, client sites, or public locations to observe credentials, intercept NFC or Bluetooth communications, or gain physical access to secure areas by tailgating authenticated employees. Relevant in the context of open-plan trading floors and client-facing banking premises with mixed public and staff access zones. NOTE: This threat is addressed by the physical security programme (visitor management, tailgating controls, manned reception, CCTV, access card audit). It is retained in this taxonomy for completeness and cross-programme traceability but has no corresponding technical endpoint ZTA control.
MITRE ID: T1056.002 – Input Capture: GUI Input Capture; T1200 – Hardware Additions
Change Log: WS2-D: Scope annotation added. Physical tailgating has no technical ZTA endpoint control. Retained for cross-programme traceability with explicit physical security programme attribution.

Key Threats: MDM Enrolment Bypass and Fake Compliance Signalling: Attackers enrol unmanaged or non-compliant devices into MDM using stolen or synthetic enrolment credentials, or manipulate device compliance reporting to present false-positive device health signals to the Conditional Access policy engine. A device presenting as compliant while operating outside enforced security policy undermines the integrity of the Zero-Trust device verification pillar entirely.
MITRE ID: T1078 – Valid Accounts; T1553 – Subvert Trust Controls

Key Threats: MDM Certificate Pinning Bypass and Check-In Interception: MDM client implementations that do not enforce certificate pinning for the MDM management channel are susceptible to MitM interception of MDM check-in communications. An attacker intercepting the check-in channel can replay compliant device posture responses to the MDM server, suppressing non-compliance signals and policy enforcement actions without modifying the endpoint itself.
MITRE ID: T1557 – Adversary-in-the-Middle; T1562.001 – Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
Change Log: WS3-C: Threat retained S2.1.14. DP-08 EXTENDED: Add mutual TLS for MDM check-in channels, certificate pinning validation, and check-in anomaly alerting. Layer 14 MD update required.

Key Threats: Intune Graph API Abuse via Overprivileged Service Principals: Service principals granted DeviceManagementConfiguration.ReadWrite.All or equivalent Microsoft Graph API scopes — commonly used in CI/CD and automation pipelines — provide the ability to read device compliance state, modify configuration profiles, deploy scripts to enrolled endpoints, and reassign device ownership across the entire Intune-managed estate. Compromise of these credentials via endpoint exposure or pipeline misconfiguration effectively provides MDM-level control over the corporate laptop fleet.
MITRE ID: T1078.004 – Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts; T1528 – Steal Application Access Token
Change Log: WS3-D: Threat retained S2.1.14. NEW CONTROL REQUIRED: DP-11 (Intune Graph API governance) to be authored in Layer 14 MD. Controls: Service principal DeviceManagementConfiguration scope restriction, API access logging, PIM-equivalent lifecycle for service principals.

Key Threats: Jailbroken or Rooted Device Enrolment: Endpoints with disabled Secure Boot, bypassed SIP (macOS), or rooted OS presenting false device health attestation to MDM compliance checks. Standard MDM compliance policies relying solely on self-reported OS integrity signals without hardware-backed attestation (TPM attestation on Windows, Apple Silicon Secure Enclave attestation on macOS) can be deceived by sufficiently modified endpoints.
MITRE ID: T1553 – Subvert Trust Controls; T1562.010 – Impair Defenses: Downgrade Attack

Key Threats: Firmware TPM Vulnerabilities and Attestation Integrity: Firmware-based TPM (fTPM) implementations are susceptible to software-exploitable vulnerabilities including CVE-2023-1017 and CVE-2023-1018 affecting TPM 2.0 reference implementations, enabling out-of-bounds read/write within the TPM context. Endpoints relying on fTPM for device health attestation, BitLocker key sealing, or WHfB credential protection should be assessed against current fTPM firmware versions. Discrete hardware TPM remains more resilient.
MITRE ID: T1542.001 – Pre-OS Boot: System Firmware; T1600 – Weaken Encryption

Key Threats: SCCM and Configuration Manager Attack Paths: Microsoft SCCM (now Microsoft Endpoint Configuration Manager) deployment infrastructure presents multiple documented high-impact attack paths catalogued in the SpecterOps "Misconfiguration Manager" (2024) research. These include NTLM relay via automatic client push installation, credential extraction from the site database, and privilege escalation to SCCM Site Admin enabling arbitrary script deployment to all managed endpoints in the hierarchy.
MITRE ID: T1072 – Software Deployment Tools; T1557.001 – Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay; T1078 – Valid Accounts
Change Log: WS3-E: Threat retained S2.1.14. NEW CONTROL REQUIRED: DP-12 (SCCM/ConfigMgr attack path hardening) to be authored in Layer 14 MD. Controls: SCCM hierarchy site server hardening, Network Access Account elimination, PXE boot authentication, admin console access via PAM.

Key Threats: Device Compliance Policy Gap Exploitation: Gaps in MDM compliance policy definitions — such as absence of checks for EDR agent health, firewall state, disk encryption enforcement, OS patch level, or screen lock timeout — allow non-compliant endpoints to satisfy device health checks and receive Conditional Access grants. Compliance policy must be treated as a living security control subject to continuous review against the current threat landscape.
MITRE ID: T1562.001 – Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools; T1553 – Subvert Trust Controls

Key Threats: Unmanaged and BYOD Device Access to Corporate Resources: Personal or contractor-owned devices accessing corporate SaaS platforms, email, or data repositories outside of MDM enrolment and device compliance enforcement. Unmanaged devices provide no device health signal to the Zero-Trust policy engine and should be restricted to MAM-only (Mobile Application Management) access with enforced data containment, or denied access to sensitive data classifications entirely per NIST SP 800-207.
MITRE ID: T1078 – Valid Accounts; T1530 – Data from Cloud Storage

Key Threats: Conditional Access Policy Misconfiguration and Gap Exploitation: Incomplete or incorrectly scoped Conditional Access policies leaving administrative portals, legacy authentication protocols, service principal sign-ins, or workload identities outside of enforcement scope. Legacy authentication protocols (SMTP AUTH, IMAP, POP3, Basic Auth) bypass all modern Conditional Access controls and must be explicitly blocked. The CA What-If tool can be used by an attacker with read-sufficient Entra ID access to enumerate policy gaps and craft authentication flows that satisfy policy conditions without meeting security intent.
MITRE ID: T1078 – Valid Accounts; T1556 – Modify Authentication Process

Key Threats: Entra ID Privileged Identity Management Bypass: Attackers with compromised eligible role assignment accounts activating Global Administrator, Privileged Role Administrator, or high-impact built-in roles via PIM without triggering approval workflows — exploiting misconfigured approval requirements, approval delegation to compromised accounts, or PIM role assignment policy gaps. PIM without enforced approval, justification, and independent audit for tier-0 roles does not satisfy Zero-Trust least-privilege principles for privileged access per NIST SP 800-207 §3.3.
MITRE ID: T1078.004 – Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts; T1098 – Account Manipulation

Key Threats: Policy Decision Point and Policy Enforcement Point Integrity: Per NIST SP 800-207 §3.1, the ZTA Policy Decision Point (PDP) and Policy Enforcement Point (PEP) are the authoritative trust arbitration components. Compromise of PDP infrastructure — including Entra ID tenant configuration, Conditional Access policy definitions, and named location and trusted network definitions — enables silent policy modification that grants access to all resources governed by the affected policy scope without individual resource compromise.
MITRE ID: T1484.002 – Domain Policy Modification: Domain Trust Modification; T1556 – Modify Authentication Process
Change Log: WS1-A (residual): "Identity; Network" → "Identity; Networks"

Key Threats: Security Tool and SIEM Configuration Tampering: Modification of SIEM alert rules, EDR policy exclusions, DLP policy suppression, or audit log forwarding configuration by privileged insiders or compromised administrator accounts to suppress detection of ongoing compromise activity. Changes to security tooling configuration must be treated as tier-0 privileged operations subject to independent approval, immutable audit logging, and automated alerting on modification.
MITRE ID: T1562.001 – Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools; T1562.002 – Impair Defenses: Disable Windows Event Logging; T1211 – Exploitation for Defense Evasion

Key Threats: Administrative Credential Theft Targeting Management Plane: Credential theft specifically targeting Global Administrators, Intune Administrators, Security Administrators, and Conditional Access Administrators whose permissions span the entire management plane. Compromise of a single unprotected Global Admin account provides effective control over all Zero-Trust enforcement infrastructure — Conditional Access, MDM compliance policy, identity governance, and security monitoring — simultaneously. Phishing-resistant MFA and privileged access workstations (PAW) are mandatory controls for this account tier.
MITRE ID: T1078.004 – Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts; T1556 – Modify Authentication Process; T1528 – Steal Application Access Token

Key Threats: Entra ID Tenant and Federation Trust Manipulation: Modification of federated identity provider trust configuration, addition of unauthorised domain federations, or manipulation of Entra ID B2B cross-tenant access settings to introduce attacker-controlled identity providers capable of issuing valid tokens accepted by the tenant. Documented in nation-state campaigns targeting cloud identity infrastructure as a durable persistence mechanism that survives password resets and MFA re-enrolment.
MITRE ID: T1484.002 – Domain Policy Modification: Domain Trust Modification; T1556.006 – Modify Authentication Process: Multi-Factor Authentication

Key Threats: Audit Log Tampering and Log Pipeline Integrity Attacks: Deletion, modification, or suppression of Entra ID audit logs, M365 Unified Audit Log entries, Intune device audit events, or SIEM-ingested endpoint telemetry to conceal management plane changes, privilege escalation, or policy modification. Microsoft 365 audit log retention periods and log forwarding pipeline integrity must be treated as security-critical controls — log gaps must trigger automated alerting rather than silent failure.
MITRE ID: T1562.008 – Impair Defenses: Disable Cloud Logs; T1070 – Indicator Removal

Key Threats: Zero-Trust Policy Engine Drift and Configuration Regression: Gradual degradation of Zero-Trust enforcement posture through accumulated policy exceptions, emergency access account proliferation, break-glass procedure abuse, and undocumented configuration changes made outside formal change management processes. Policy drift is a documented failure mode for mature ZTA deployments and requires continuous automated configuration compliance scanning against approved policy baseline — equivalent to infrastructure-as-code drift detection applied to identity and access policy.
MITRE ID: T1078 – Valid Accounts; T1484 – Domain Policy Modification

Key Threats: Supply Chain Compromise of Management Plane Tooling: Compromise of identity governance platforms, PAM solutions, MDM management consoles, or SIEM platforms used to administer the Zero-Trust policy engine via software supply chain attack or malicious update. Management plane tooling represents a single-point-of-failure for the entire ZTA enforcement architecture and must be subject to the same supply chain integrity controls applied to endpoint software, including SBOM tracking, integrity verification, and vendor security assessment.
MITRE ID: T1195.002 – Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain; T1072 – Software Deployment Tools
